text: The surgeon performs a robotic sigmoid resection for diverticulitis, beginning with a medial to lateral dissection. The ureter is visible, and the superior hemorrhoidal vessel is isolated. The patient has a thin, redundant colon, facilitating the procedure. The splenic flexure is not mobilized.

The surgeon continues dissection up to the descending colon, performing limited rectal mobilization to facilitate an attention-free anastomosis. The rectal mesentery is dissected to clean the distal margin, followed by dissection of the proximal mesentery while preserving the left colic artery, visible next to the vessel sealer.

The surgeon completes the division of the mesentery at the proximal distal margin and uses indocyanine green (ICG) to mark the area with cautery, making it visible under white light. The rectum is then divided.

The surgeon divides the proximal colon and performs a transrectal extraction of the intact specimen without using a wound protector. An end-to-end anastomosis is initiated with a single layer technique, using a threosilk stay suture to stabilize the lateral aspect. A running stitch is applied along the posterior inner row, starting in the middle and moving to the left side.

The surgeon uses three OV locks to perform a running over-and-over stitch starting in the middle, adjacent to the first suture, and continues around the near side. The anterior row is then sutured, with the stay suture aiding in manipulating the bowel for optimal angles and visibility during the procedure.

The surgeon completes the closure of the anastomosis by running sutures over and back, ensuring they cross each other before tying them together. A leak test is performed using a 19mm proctoscope, which is successfully passed through the anastomosis without difficulty.
